title: MIND-V: Hierarchical World Model for Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation with RL-based Physical Alignment
authors: Ruicheng Zhang; Mingyang Zhang; Jun Zhou; Xiaofan Liu; Zunnan Xu; Zhizhou Zhong; Puxin Yan; Haocheng Luo; Xiu Li
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2512.06628 in Dec 2025; supplied revision v4 dated 5 Jul 2026
open_source_notes: No public implementation or model release was identified in the supplied manuscript.
task_domain: Long-Horizon Manipulation; Video World Simulation; Synthetic Data Generation
primary_problem: World Modeling; Hierarchical Planning; Video Generation; Policy Learning
world_model_detail: A Gemini-based Semantic Reasoning Hub plans subtasks, a Behavioral Semantic Bridge grounds them into masks and trajectories, and a CogVideoX-based diffusion generator synthesizes video; V-JEPA 2 supplies a physical-coherence reward.
action_conditioned: No / Indirect — conditioned on planned affordances, masks, and trajectories rather than low-level robot actions
planning_method: Language Planner; Hierarchical; Visual Rollout Selection
planning_representation: Symbolic subtask tuples; affordance masks; trajectories; generated video rollouts
horizon_basis: Long-horizon evaluations contain two to four subtasks, while the autoregressive subtask design supports arbitrarily long sequences.
replanning_online: Yes — staged visual future rollouts select or refine candidate continuations at each subtask transition
learning_type: Supervised Fine-Tuning; GRPO Reinforcement Learning; Foundation-Model Adaptation
pretraining: Yes — Gemini 2.5 Pro, Affordance-R1, CogVideoX-5B, and V-JEPA 2
observation_modality: RGB scene image; language; object and robot masks; planned trajectories
goal_representation: Natural-language instruction decomposed into ordered subtasks
action_space: Structured affordance and trajectory conditions for video generation
benchmark: Bridge V2 and unseen web scenes; MimicGen Coffee, StackThree, and Square policy-learning tasks
robot_platform: Bridge V2 embodiments and MimicGen simulated manipulators
main_contribution: Introduces a hierarchical video world model that autonomously converts high-level instructions into physically aligned, long-horizon manipulation-video rollouts.
novelty: Combines VLM task decomposition, structured semantic-to-pixel conditioning, V-JEPA-based physical-reward alignment, and propose-verify-refine visual rollouts.
limitations: Staged rollout selection adds substantial inference overhead, and errors in upstream affordance localization can propagate through all downstream generation stages.
future_work: Improve inference efficiency, affordance grounding robustness, and physical fidelity while extending generated data to broader real robotic domains.
evidence_world_model: The Motor Video Generator predicts future manipulation videos, while V-JEPA 2 evaluates their latent physical coherence.
evidence_planning: The Semantic Reasoning Hub decomposes tasks and generates multiple candidate continuations that are evaluated and refined at subtask boundaries.
evidence_horizon: Long-horizon tasks contain two to four linked subtasks, and 111-frame three-subtask videos are generated autoregressively.
review_flags: Core hierarchical video world model. Its planner operates in synthetic video space and does not directly output executable robot actions. Record year as 2025 despite the supplied 2026 revision.
source_document: MIND-V_ Hierarchical Video Generation for Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation with RL-based Physical Alignment.txt

title: Multi-robot geometric task-and-motion planning for collaborative manipulation tasks
authors: Hejia Zhang; Shao-Hung Chan; Jie Zhong; Jiaoyang Li; Peter Kolapo; Sven Koenig; Zach Agioutantis; Steven Schafrik; Stefanos Nikolaidis
open_source_notes: No paper-specific code release was identified in the supplied article.
task_domain: Multi-Robot Manipulation; Geometric Task and Motion Planning; Mining Robotics
world_model_detail: N/A — uses explicit reachability, occlusion, task-skeleton, and motion-planning models rather than learned dynamics.
planning_method: MCTS; Mixed-Integer Programming; Geometric Task and Motion Planning
planning_representation: Hybrid: collaborative manipulation graph, symbolic task skeleton, object placements, and robot trajectories
horizon_basis: Reported benchmark plans generally contain roughly three to five synchronized task stages, with each stage grounded into multi-robot motion; roof-bolting executions last 250–270 seconds.
learning_type: None — search, optimization, and motion planning
observation_modality: Fully observed structured geometry, object regions, reachability, and occlusion relations
goal_representation: Goal regions for target objects and task-specific mining objectives
action_space: Hybrid: robot-object assignments, relocation and handover actions, object placements, and continuous trajectories
benchmark: Pick-and-place arms domain; mobile-manipulator box-moving domain; two underground roof-bolting scenarios
robot_platform: Multiple robotic arms and mobile manipulators; ABB arm coordinated with an autonomous roof bolter
main_contribution: Builds collaborative manipulation graphs and uses MIP-guided MCTS to find efficient multi-robot geometric task-and-motion plans.
novelty: Precomputes robot-specific reachability and occlusion information to generate promising collaborative task skeletons, including handovers, before grounding their continuous parameters.
limitations: Assumes monotone object motion, predefined handover regions, synchronous actions, and full observability; it does not optimize trajectory length or execution time directly.
future_work: Support non-monotone tasks, discover handover regions dynamically, account for sensing uncertainty and trajectory cost, use learning to accelerate planning, and extend to broader MR-TAMP problems.
evidence_world_model: All environmental reasoning uses explicit geometric and symbolic structures, not a learned predictive transition model.
evidence_planning: MIPs propose task skeletons, MCTS balances exploration and exploitation, and motion planners ground object placements and robot trajectories.
evidence_horizon: The reported task skeletons are multi-stage but relatively compact; the paper is not explicitly framed as a long-horizon planning method.
review_flags: Core multi-robot TAMP baseline. Keep separate from the explicitly long-horizon 113-part construction planner in batch 7.
source_document: Multi-robot geometric task-and-motion planning for collaborative manipulation tasks.txt

title: Octo: An Open-Source Generalist Robot Policy
authors: Octo Model Team (Dibya Ghosh; Homer Walke; Karl Pertsch; Kevin Black; Oier Mees; et al.)
year_basis: Published at Robotics: Science and Systems 2024
open_source_notes: Training code, finetuning code, model checkpoints, and data infrastructure are publicly released.
task_domain: Manipulation; Generalist Robot Policies; Cross-Embodiment Learning
primary_problem: Policy Learning; Foundation Model; Efficient Finetuning
world_model_detail: N/A — Octo directly maps task and observation tokens to future action chunks.
horizon_basis: Octo is tested on long-horizon tasks such as Stanford Coffee, but no explicit search, world-model rollout, or high-level decomposition is used.
replanning_online: Yes — the policy repeatedly generates action chunks from updated observations
learning_type: Cross-Embodiment Imitation Learning; Diffusion Action Modeling; Foundation-Policy Pretraining
policy_type: Generalist Robot Policy; Diffusion Action Policy
pretraining: Yes — trained on 800,000 Open X-Embodiment trajectories
observation_modality: Multiple RGB cameras; proprioception; optional new modalities such as force-torque after finetuning
action_space: Flexible end-effector, joint-position, single-arm, and bimanual action spaces
benchmark: Nine real robot setups including WidowX BridgeV2, UR5 Tabletop, CMU Baking, Stanford Coffee, Berkeley Insertion, Pick-Up, Coke, and Bimanual
robot_platform: Nine real robot setups across four institutions
main_contribution: Provides a fully open-source transformer policy that supports multiple robots, observations, task specifications, and action spaces and can be efficiently finetuned.
novelty: Combines a flexible token-based transformer, language or goal-image conditioning, diffusion action chunks, and adapter-based finetuning across new robot interfaces.
limitations: Performance is limited by sparse wrist-camera and language annotations, degrades for novel skills and scenes, and is trained only from optimal demonstration data on manipulation platforms.
future_work: Improve wrist-camera and language conditioning, learn from suboptimal or online interaction data, and expand to navigation and mobile manipulation.
evidence_world_model: The model predicts robot actions directly and contains no learned transition or future-observation model.
evidence_planning: Long-horizon tasks are handled through repeated closed-loop policy inference rather than an explicit planner.
evidence_horizon: The evaluation explicitly includes long task horizons such as Stanford Coffee, but planning_horizon remains None.
review_flags: Core generalist-policy baseline; exclude from world-model and explicit-planning counts.
source_document: Octo_ An Open-Source Generalist Robot Policy.txt

title: Open X-Embodiment: Robotic Learning Datasets and RT-X Models
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2310.08864 in Oct 2023; supplied revision v9 dated 14 May 2025
publication_status: Dataset and model paper / revised preprint
open_source_notes: The standardized datasets, sample code, project resources, and RT-1-X model are publicly released.
primary_problem: Dataset; Generalist Policy Learning; Cross-Embodiment Transfer
long_horizon: N/A — the paper aggregates heterogeneous tasks rather than evaluating one normalized horizon
language_conditioned: N/A — many constituent datasets and RT-X models use language instructions
world_model_detail: N/A — RT-1-X and RT-2-X are direct robot policies rather than predictive environment models.
prediction_target: Robot actions for the accompanying RT-X policy experiments
horizon_basis: The dataset spans diverse tasks and embodiments, but the paper does not define a single planning horizon.
learning_type: Dataset Aggregation; Cross-Embodiment Imitation Learning; Vision-Language-Action Fine-Tuning
pretraining: Yes — RT-X models are trained on the aggregated cross-embodiment dataset
observation_modality: Multiple RGB, proprioceptive, language, and embodiment-specific sensor configurations
goal_representation: Natural-language instruction and dataset-specific task labels
action_space: Standardized heterogeneous robot action spaces across embodiments
benchmark: More than one million real-robot episodes from 22 embodiments, 21 institutions, 527 skills, and 160,266 tasks
main_contribution: Consolidates a large cross-institution robot dataset and demonstrates positive cross-embodiment transfer with RT-1-X and RT-2-X.
novelty: Standardizes heterogeneous datasets and shows that policies trained jointly across robots can outperform embodiment-specific models and gain emergent skills.
limitations: The study does not cover highly different sensing or actuation modalities, does not test adaptation to entirely new robots, and lacks a criterion predicting when cross-embodiment transfer will help.
future_work: Expand embodiment and modality diversity, study transfer to unseen robots, and develop principles for predicting positive or negative cross-domain transfer.
evidence_world_model: The accompanying RT-X models map observations and instructions directly to actions without modeling future environment dynamics.
evidence_planning: The contribution is dataset aggregation and direct policy learning, not search or task decomposition.
evidence_horizon: No corpus-wide task horizon is defined because the dataset merges many independent robot datasets and skills.
review_flags: First Dataset row in the taxonomy. Exclude from method, survey, benchmark, world-model, and planner counts; retain for dataset-scale and foundation-policy analyses.
source_document: Open X-Embodiment_ Robotic Learning Datasets and RT-X Models.txt

title: PaLM-E: An Embodied Multimodal Language Model
authors: Danny Driess; Fei Xia; Mehdi S. M. Sajjadi; Corey Lynch; Aakanksha Chowdhery; Brian Ichter; Ayzaan Wahid; Jonathan Tompson; Quan Vuong; Tianhe Yu; Wenlong Huang; Yevgen Chebotar; Pierre Sermanet; Daniel Duckworth; Sergey Levine; Vincent Vanhoucke; Karol Hausman; Marc Toussaint; Klaus Greff; Andy Zeng; Igor Mordatch; Pete Florence
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2303.03378 on 6 Mar 2023
open_source_notes: A project website and demonstrations are available, but a complete public model and training-code release was not established.
task_domain: Embodied AI; Manipulation; Mobile Manipulation; Visual Question Answering
primary_problem: Language Planning; Multimodal Foundation Models; Embodied Reasoning
human_robot_interaction: Yes — receives human instructions and supports grounded dialogue
world_model_detail: N/A — multimodal sensor tokens condition a language model that emits textual plans, without learning action-conditioned environment dynamics.
prediction_target: Textual answers, subgoals, and sequential robot-plan steps
planning_representation: Textual subgoals conditioned on image, state, neural 3D, language, and plan history
horizon_basis: PaLM-E autoregressively emits multi-step subgoals for real tabletop and mobile-manipulation tasks until termination.
replanning_online: Yes — the next plan step is conditioned on current visual observations and previous execution history
learning_type: Multitask Supervised Fine-Tuning; Multimodal Foundation-Model Transfer
policy_type: Embodied Multimodal LLM with External Low-Level Skill Policies
pretraining: Yes — PaLM language model, vision transformers, and multimodal encoders
observation_modality: Language; RGB images; continuous state estimates; neural 3D scene representations
action_space: Discrete textual subgoals mapped to pretrained manipulation and navigation policies
benchmark: TAMP environment; Language-Table; real mobile manipulation; OK-VQA; VQAv2; COCO captioning
robot_platform: Language-Table tabletop robot; Everyday Robots mobile manipulator; simulated TAMP environment
main_contribution: Injects continuous perceptual and state representations directly into a pretrained language model to create one multimodal model for embodied planning and general vision-language reasoning.
novelty: Demonstrates positive transfer from internet-scale language and vision tasks to data-efficient, multi-embodiment robotic planning using a single end-to-end model.
limitations: Planning remains bounded by the available low-level skill policies and training mixture, and the model does not explicitly predict physical dynamics or guarantee safe and feasible actions.
future_work: Combine embodied language modeling with stronger large-scale visual and geometric representations, more capable low-level control, and improved grounding and safety.
evidence_world_model: PaLM-E grounds sensor inputs inside an LLM but does not predict next environment states conditioned on candidate actions.
evidence_planning: It generates the next textual plan step from current observations and history, then maps that subgoal to a low-level robot policy.
evidence_horizon: The paper demonstrates real long-horizon block sorting and mobile manipulation under disturbances through repeated subgoal generation.
review_flags: Core multimodal language-planning paper; not a world model and not a direct low-level policy.
source_document: PaLM-E_ An Embodied Multimodal Language Model.txt
